transcript:
Here's a network of nodes around a central hub, all dark and dormant at first — then a light sweeps out from the center, and one by one the nodes catch fire in orange, like something waking up in sequence.

That sweep isn't decorative — look at the timing. Every line and shape has its own "begin" delay, offset by fractions of a second, so the activation ripples outward rather than snapping on all at once. And if you look at the group names in the code — "awaiting invocation left," "callback nest," "promise chain right" — the artist is borrowing the actual vocabulary of code execution. There's even text drifting through labeled "parsing..." and "calling," fading in and out. The piece isn't just depicting a network lighting up; it's staging its own source code's lifecycle, node by node, as if you're watching a function tree get called.

So the pulsing rings you see aren't just visual rhythm — the code literally calls them "invocation ripples," proof rendered on screen that something external is reading this file right now. Which means the piece only really exists in the moment you're watching it.

cues.context: you look at the group names in the code — "awaiting
